messages.content:
Can you please correct these lines of code below.
wscf is always Text in a cell and the name of a excel sheet.
What will be the Dim as  wscf.
Can for wscfw can you also give me two ways of identifying a sheet
The first using the Active Sheet
And the scond using the sheet name Sheet1,

Dim wscfw As Worksheet
Dim wscfr As Range
Dim wscf As 

Set wscfw = Workbook.Worksheets.ActiveSheet
Set wscfr = wscfw.Range("I2")
Set wscf =  wscfr.Value

Dim wscfw As Worksheet
Dim wscfr As Range
Dim wscf As String

Set wscfw = ActiveSheet 'Identifying sheet using Active Sheet
Set wscfw = Sheets("Sheet1") 'Identifying using sheet name

Set wscfr = wscfw.Range("I2")
wscf = wscfr.Value

In the VBA page

what does Option Explict do to the page and what are the differences between;

Public Sub xxx()
Private Sub xxx()
Sub xxx()

and when should you and should you not use them

Option Explicit is a statement used at the top of a VBA module before any Sub or Function statements. It enforces the declaration of all variables, which means that all variables must be declared before their first use in the procedure. 

Declaring variables explicitly can help identify coding errors, force developers to think through their code, and allow for a smoother debugging process.

Public Sub xxx() declares a public procedure that can be accessed from any module in the project and by other projects that reference the module.

Private Sub xxx() declares a private procedure that can only be accessed by code within the module in which it is declared.

Sub xxx() declares a procedure that is accessible to all modules in the project, but only if it is declared as public.

You should use Public or Private when you want to control the scope of the procedure. If you want a procedure to be accessible outside the module in which it is declared, make it Public. If you want a procedure to only be accessible within the module in which it is declared, make it Private. You can omit the declaration statement altogether (Sub xxx()), but this is generally discouraged as it can lead to scope and typing errors. It is better to declare all variables explicitly to prevent coding errors.
Can you give me a simple example of why a procedure may need to be accessible outside the module in which it is declared

Sure, here's an example:

Let's say you have a VBA project with two modules: Module1 and Module2. In Module1, you have a Public Sub procedure called runReport. This procedure retrieves data from a database, formats it, and then outputs it into an Excel worksheet.

In Module2, you have some code that needs to run the runReport procedure every time a button is clicked. You can call the runReport procedure from Module2 because it is declared as Public in Module1.

If the runReport procedure was declared as Private in Module1, it would not be accessible from Module2 and you would have to duplicate the code from the runReport procedure in Module2 or create a new Public procedure in Module1 that would call the runReport procedure.

Declaring the runReport procedure as Public in Module1 allows you to reuse the code throughout the project and makes it easier to maintain because you only have to modify it in one place.

I have VBA coding for a page that start with the Private Sub Worksheet_Activate() routine and ends with the Private Sub Worksheet_Deactivate() routine. It is a large page and will be exactly the same for 50 other pages. Is there a way where the code can sit in one place and ever

Yes, there is a way to place the code in one place and have it executed for multiple sheets. You can create a VBA module and place the code inside a Public subroutine, and then call this subroutine from each worksheet using the Worksheet_Activate and Worksheet_Deactivate events. Here's an example:

1. Open the Visual Basic Editor in Excel (press ALT+F11).
2. Insert a new module (Insert->Module) in the project.
3. Copy the code from the Worksheet_Activate() to Worksheet_Deactivate() private subroutines and paste them into a new Public subroutine in the module.
4. In each worksheet, delete the code from the Worksheet_Activate and Worksheet_Deactivate subroutines.
5. In each worksheet, add the following code to the code window:
```
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    Call MySub 'replace "MySub" with the name of the public subroutine you created in the module
End Sub

Private Sub Worksheet_Deactivate()
    Call MySub 'replace "MySub" with the name of the public subroutine you created in the module
End Sub
```
6. Save the changes to the module and the worksheet VBA code.

Now, when any of the worksheets are activated or deactivated, the MySub routine from the module will be executed. This allows you to write the code once and have it applied to multiple worksheets.

If I've got it, I copy the entire code from my so called master sheet and paste it into a module. The  Worksheet_Activate  Routine in the Module I rename it to a Public Sub () routine. The same for all the other subs in the module. Lets say for example the Private Sub Worksheet_Activate() I change to Public Sub Start (), and the Private Sub Worksheet_Change() I change to Public Sub Change() and the Private Sub Worksheet_Deactivate(), I change to Public Sub Stop(). The in my sheets, Where I have Private Sub Worksheet_Activate() Call Start, where I have Private Sub Worksheet_Change(), I call Change and where I have Private Sub Worksheet_Deactivate(), I call Stop.

Yes, that's correct! Here are the steps you can follow:

1. Copy the entire code from your master sheet and paste it into a new module.
2. Rename the Private Sub Worksheet_Activate() to Public Sub Start().
3. Rename the Private Sub Worksheet_Change() to Public Sub Change().
4. Rename the Private Sub Worksheet_Deactivate() to Public Sub Stop().
5. In each worksheet, delete the code from the Worksheet_Activate, Worksheet_Change, and Worksheet_Deactivate subroutines.
6. In each worksheet, add the following code to the code window:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    Call Start 'Call the Start subroutine from the module
End Sub

Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
    Call Change(Target) 'Call the Change subroutine from the module and pass the Target range as an argument
End Sub

Private Sub Worksheet_Deactivate()
    Call Stop 'Call the Stop subroutine from the module
End Sub
```

7. Save the changes to the module and the worksheet VBA code.

This will allow you to write the code once and have it applied to multiple worksheets by calling the subroutines from the module.
What about Subs that call on other subs

If you have subs in your master sheet that call other subs, you will need to ensure that those other subs are also included in the module and declared as Public, as the Private subs will not be accessible from the worksheet anymore.

For example, let's say you have a Private subroutine called DoSomething() in your master sheet that is called by the Worksheet_Activate() subroutine. DoSomething() also calls another Private subroutine called DoSomethingElse(). Here's an example of how you could modify it to work with the new structure:

1. Copy all the subs from your master sheet to the module.
2. Rename the Worksheet_Activate() to Public Sub Start().
3. Rename the DoSomething() to Public Sub DoSomething().
4. Rename the DoSomethingElse() to Public Sub DoSomethingElse().
5. In the Start() subroutine, replace the call to DoSomething() with the call to Module2.DoSomething() (replace Module2 with the name of the module).
6. In the DoSomething() subroutine, replace the call to DoSomethingElse() with the call to Module2.DoSomethingElse().
7. In each worksheet, delete the code from the Worksheet_Activate, Worksheet_Change, and Worksheet_Deactivate subroutines.
8. In each worksheet, add the following code to the code window:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    Call Module2.Start 'Call the Start subroutine from the module
End Sub
```

9. Save the changes to the module and the worksheet VBA code.

This will allow you to write the code once and have it applied to multiple worksheets by calling the subroutines from the module, including the subs that call other subs. Just make sure all the subs are declared as Public in the module.
now that I have copied the sheet vba to a module I am getting an invalid use of  the Me Key,  ColLis Me.Range("B1")

The "Me" keyword is used to refer to the current object in which the code is running. When you move your code from a worksheet to a module, you can no longer use the "Me" keyword because there is no longer a worksheet object associated with the code.

To resolve this error, replace the "Me" keyword with a direct reference to the worksheet object.

For example, if you have code like this in your master sheet:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    ColList Me.Range("B1")
End Sub
```

You can modify it to look like this in the module:

```
Public Sub Start()
    ColList Worksheets("Sheet1").Range("B1") 'Replace "Sheet1" with the name of your worksheet
End Sub
```

Alternatively, you can pass the worksheet object as a parameter to the subroutine in the module. Here's an example:

```
Public Sub Start(ws as Worksheet)
    ColList ws.Range("B1")
End Sub
```

Then, in each worksheet, you can call the Start() subroutine by passing the worksheet object as an argument:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    Call Start(Me) 'Pass the worksheet object to the Start subroutine
End Sub
``` 

Note: For the second method to work, you will need to modify the other subroutines in your module to accept the worksheet object as a parameter.
ColList Worksheets(“Sheet1”).Range(“B1”)  can you change this to ColList ActiveSheet

Yes, you can change that line to `ColList ActiveSheet.Range("B1")`. This will use the currently active worksheet instead of specifying a specific worksheet by name (like "Sheet1"). 

Just be aware that, if your code is run from a different worksheet (other than the one where the code is stored), ActiveSheet will reference that worksheet instead of the one where the code is stored.
I have used the ActiveSheet approach. But it now gives me an error that the ColList is not defined

The error message "ColList is not defined" means that VBA is not recognizing the name "ColList". This can happen for a few reasons:

1. Typing error: Double-check that you have spelled "ColList" correctly in your code. Make sure the capitalization and spacing matches the name of your subroutine.

2. Missing module: If "ColList" is a subroutine in another module, make sure that module is also included in your project and that it is not missing.

3. Access level: If "ColList" is a Private subroutine in your master sheet, it will not be accessible from a module. In this case, you would need to change the access level to "Public" so that it can be accessed from other parts of the project.

4. Missing code: If "ColList" is not a subroutine in your code, you will need to create it. 

Here's an example of how to define a ColList subroutine:

```
Public Sub ColList(rng As Range)
    'Do something with the range here
End Sub
```

Make sure that the name and parameters match the name of the subroutine you are calling in your code.

If Target.Column = 5 Then
ColList Target                                                   Public Sub ColList(rng As Range)           there is an error.     It appears as if the ColList Target does not like the ColList(rng Range). Any suggestions

The error you're seeing may be related to the fact that "ColList" is not a function that returns a value, but rather a subroutine that performs some action. You cannot use ColList Target as an expression in an "if" statement because ColList doesn't return a value.

If you want to call the ColList subroutine when a cell in column 5 changes, you can call it directly from the Worksheet_Change event handler, like this:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
    If Target.Column = 5 Then
        ColList Target
    End If
End Sub
```

This will call the ColList subroutine and pass the Target range as an argument only if the changed cell is in column 5.

To define the ColList subroutine, you can do something like this:

```
Public Sub ColList(rng As Range)
    'Do something with the range here
    Debug.Print rng.Value 'Print the value of the changed cell to the Immediate Window
End Sub
```

This will print the value of the changed cell to the Immediate Window for testing purposes. You can replace this with whatever action you want the ColList subroutine to perform.
The ColList Target is currently being called in the Worksheet_Change routine just as you have described

If you're calling ColList Target in the Worksheet_Change routine, and getting an error, it's possible that the error is due to the way you're passing the "Target" variable to the ColList subroutine.

When you call ColList Target, the Target variable is being passed as an argument to the ColList subroutine. However, the ColList subroutine expects a range type argument. You can fix this by checking the "Target" variable's TypeName in the Worksheet_Change sub to ensure that it is, in fact, a Range object before passing it to the ColList sub. Here's an example:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
    If Target.Column = 5 Then
        If TypeName(Target) = "Range" Then 'Ensure that Target is a Range object
            ColList Target
        End If 
    End If
End Sub
```

By wrapping the ColList call inside an TypeName check on the Target variable, you can ensure that the ColList subroutine only receives a valid range object. 

Make sure that the ColList subroutine is defined correctly with a Range argument.
ByRef Argument Type Mismatch is the rror I am getting

The "ByRef argument type mismatch" error means that the argument types being passed to the ColList subroutine do not match the argument types defined in the subroutine. 

The ColList subroutine should have a Range type argument, but the Target argument passed to the subroutine in the Worksheet_Change event might not be a Range.

Try declaring the Target variable explicitly as a Range at the beginning of the Worksheet_Change event, like this:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
    Dim rng As Range
    Set rng = Target
    If rng.Column = 5 Then
        ColList rng
    End If
End Sub
```

By declaring the Target variable as Range, you ensure that it is properly recognized as a range type for the purposes of the ColList subroutine. 

Also, double-check that the ColList subroutine has a single argument that is declared as a Range. If the argument is declared as a different type, such as "Variant" or "Object", this could also cause a type mismatch error.
now an object is required for the Set rng = Target

The error message "object is required" suggests that the Target variable may be empty or contain an object type that is not compatible with the Range object. 

To avoid this error, you can add a check that the Target is not Nothing before trying to set it to a variable.  Here's an example:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
    If Not Target Is Nothing Then
        Dim rng As Range
        Set rng = Target
        If rng.Column = 5 Then
            ColList rng
        End If
    End If
End Sub
```

This code first checks if the Target range is empty or not. If it is not empty, it declares a Range variable "rng" and sets it to the Target range. After that, it checks if the rng variable's Column is 5. Finally, it calls the ColList subroutine using the rng variable as an argument.

By checking if the Target range is empty before setting it to the rng variable, you avoid the "object is required" error when Target contains no object.
error on th If Not Target Is Nothing Then

If you're getting an error on the "If Not Target Is Nothing" statement, it could mean that "Target" is not being recognized as a Range variable.

To fix this, try changing the line that declares the "Target" variable in the Worksheet_Change event to explicitly declare it as a Range object:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
```

This will ensure that VBA recognizes "Target" as a Range object and that the "Not Target Is Nothing" statement works correctly.
